core_claim: Miso Labs' open-weights 8B emotive text-to-speech model makes convincing voice cloning cheap and natural, so a familiar voice is no longer proof of identity and sensitive requests must be verified out-of-band.

caption:
Miso Labs released MisoTTS, an 8 billion parameter emotive text-to-speech model with open weights. It can clone a voice, and the emotion behind it, from seconds of reference audio. The license is a modified MIT, so it is broadly usable, and the model watermarks its output by default.

The security angle is not a brand-new threat, it is a cheaper and more convincing one. Open, emotive voice cloning keeps eroding the oldest trust signal we have, which is recognizing someone's voice on the phone. That signal is what vishing, fake-relative scams, and CEO wire-transfer fraud run on. The FBI has warned for a while that criminals use AI-generated voice to impersonate relatives in crisis and to impersonate officials. A default watermark helps, but a motivated abuser can strip it, so do not lean on it.

The fix is process, not detection. Treat a familiar voice as no proof of identity. Verify any sensitive or financial request out-of-band: hang up and call back a known number, or use a code word you agreed on with family and your team. For wire transfers and payment approvals, require dual authorization, not a voice on the line.

A voice you recognize is not identity anymore. Verify the request on a channel the caller cannot fake.

Does your wire-transfer approval still trust a familiar voice on the phone?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Does your team's payment approval still trust a familiar voice on the phone, or do you verify out-of-band?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): a large brass gramophone horn flaring open in the dark with a hard rim of light along its lip, with the headline in the lower third.
Slide 2 (context): a single rotating wax cylinder on a spindle in the dark, a fine groove spiraling around it and faint vapor lifting off the top.
Slide 3 (risk): a lone mockingbird on a bare branch in the dark, beak open mid-song, a thin glowing thread of sound trailing into shadow.
Slide 4 (mechanism): a metal tuning fork standing upright in the dark, its tines blurred and vibrating, fine concentric rings of sound radiating outward.
Slide 5 (failure_point): a heavy bronze bell hanging in the dark at the moment it is struck, a bright halo of vibration spreading around the rim.
Slide 6 (defense): a still dark pool of water with two drops falling at once, their concentric ripples spreading toward each other and meeting in the center.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a single vinyl record cracked clean across its grooves in deep shadow, a thin rim of light along the fracture line.
Slide 8 (cta): a narrow canyon at night with faint glowing arcs of an echo fading into empty dark space, with the comment question and handle.

sources.supports: Miso Labs released MisoTTS, an 8B emotive text-to-speech model with open weights.
8B emotive TTS, open weights under a modified MIT license, voice cloning from a short reference clip, SilentCipher watermarking by default with an explicit anti-impersonation warning.

sources.source: FBI IC3 Public Service Announcement, December 2024
sources.supports: Criminals use generative AI voice to impersonate relatives in crisis situations and to access accounts, grounding the AI-voice fraud trend.

narration.text: A familiar voice is no longer proof that it is really them.
Meesoh Labs just open sourced an 8 billion parameter emotive voice model that can clone a voice from seconds of audio.
So verify any sensitive request on a second channel. Call back a known number, or agree on a code word with family and your team.
A voice you recognize is not identity anymore. Verify the request on a channel the caller cannot fake.
Does your wire-transfer approval still trust a familiar voice on the phone? Tell me below.
